taara_karthik.name: Taara Karthik
taara_karthik.summary:
Out of Illinois, Taara Karthik has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U13 level, breaking into the semifinals 2 times. Across 7 entries in 4 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Taara is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Taara has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
taara_karthik.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Taara Karthik, from Illinois, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 7 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Taara is right on schedule. It all started at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, and so far, the only thing Taara has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Taara saga began at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Taara plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 7 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Taara has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Taara showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U11, which means Taara traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Taara entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Taara paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Taara keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Taara has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Taara, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Taara. We will be here, taking notes.

taara_kt.name: Taara Kt
taara_kt.summary:
Out of Illinois, Taara Kt is in the early chapters of a competitive journey, currently competing at the U11 level, with 4 entries across 2 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

A true triple-threat, Taara competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.
taara_kt.roast:

It all started at 2025 Egret MW CRC, and so far, the only thing Taara has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Taara saga began at 2025 Egret MW CRC with 1 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Taara plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 4 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Taara has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Taara showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U11, which means Taara traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Taara entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Taara paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Taara is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Taara wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Taara, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

tahmid_hossain.name: Tahmid Hossain
tahmid_hossain.summary:
Tahmid Hossain has collected 2 tournament titles, currently competing at the U19 level, capturing 2 titles across 1 tournaments and 3 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Tahmid keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Tahmid is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

In doubles, Tahmid has formed effective partnerships:
  • Kaitlyn Kim (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in XD
  • Matthew H Ponta (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in BD
tahmid_hossain.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Tahmid Hossain, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 2 wins across 3 entries in 1 tournaments. That is a 67% conversion rate, which means roughly 4 out of every 10 times Tahmid shows up to a tournament, Tahmid pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Tahmid saga began at 2026 CBA Midwest OLC, where Tahmid burst onto the scene with a title in XD U19 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Tahmid has delivered on some of those promises: 2 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Tahmid promised and what Tahmid has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Tahmid plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Tahmid is 0-for-1. Zero titles in 1 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Tahmid and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Tahmid needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo.

Tahmid is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Tahmid wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Tahmid, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

tailong_cheng.name: Tailong Cheng
tailong_cheng.summary:
Representing Massachusetts, Tailong Cheng has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U13 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Tailong's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Tailong is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

With 19 different doubles partners over the years, Tailong adapts to anyone:
  • Cyrus M Cai (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (7 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Arushi Saha (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #5)

Tailong has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Tailong has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Tailong brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
tailong_cheng.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Tailong Cheng, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U13 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 52 career entries in 20 tournaments, Tailong has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 51 entries where Tailong drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 52 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Tailong saga began at 2023 MassBad NE OLC with 1 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Tailong would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 6 times Tailong has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 6 times Tailong has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Tailong's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Tailong plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 52 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 19 of them, to be precise. Tailong has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Tailong's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Cyrus M Cai: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Cyrus M Cai has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BD U13 — we hope Sahir Bhuiyan has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Tailong entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Tailong's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Tailong and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Tailong has shown up 6 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Tailong is not just losing locally — Tailong is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Cheng family travels cross-country so Tailong can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Tailong has 24 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Tailong showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U13, which means Tailong traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC, where Tailong entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Tailong paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 52 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Tailong. Tailong just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Tailong has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Tailong. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Tailong: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

tanay_kankanala.name: Tanay Kankanala
tanay_kankanala.summary:
Hailing from North Carolina, Tanay Kankanala has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U15 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Tanay's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Tanay has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Tanay especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (7 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

Tanay has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 21 different players across the career:
  • Adhip Sundar (current partner) — 4 events, 1 title together in BD, spanning 4 seasons
  • Steven Lei (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #5, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Erela Liu — 2 events in XD, best: #4
  • Vihaan Darbha — a trusted BD partner (5 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

Tanay has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Tanay has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 9 seeded entries across the career, Tanay consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 6 seasons and 1 title, Tanay has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
tanay_kankanala.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Tanay Kankanala, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Tanay has 63 career entries across 26 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 2% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Tanay saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 1 entries and a best finish of 13th. The first title would not come until 2024 South OLC (Nets & Turf) in BD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Tanay's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 1 title in 24 entries — a stretch where Tanay actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Tanay is 0-for-5. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Tanay experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Tanay has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Tanay does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Tanay has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 1 wins means Tanay is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Tanay plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Tanay is 0-for-26. Zero titles in 26 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Tanay and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Tanay needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 21 of them, to be precise. Tanay has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Tanay's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Vihaan Darbha: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Vihaan Darbha has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Erela Liu has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Tanay's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Tanay has shown up 7 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Tanay has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Tanay is not just losing locally — Tanay is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Kankanala family travels cross-country so Tanay can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Tanay has 26 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Tanay showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U13, which means Tanay traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Tanay entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Tanay paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 63 entries, and 62 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Tanay looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Tanay's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Tanay has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Tanay. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

With 21 seeded entries across the career, Taneesh consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

tanishkaa_karthik.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Tanishkaa Karthik, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 6 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Tanishkaa is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, and so far, the only thing Tanishkaa has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Tanishkaa saga began at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Tanishkaa plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 6 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Tanishkaa has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Tanishkaa has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Tanishkaa showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15, which means Tanishkaa traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Tanishkaa entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Tanishkaa paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Tanishkaa is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Tanishkaa wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Tanishkaa, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

tanushri_thokala.name: Tanushri Thokala
tanushri_thokala.summary:
A North Carolina competitor, Tanushri Thokala has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U19 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Tanushri's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Tanushri has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Tanushri especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #4, a doubles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (8 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

Tanushri has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 21 different players across the career:
  • Callie Chen (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #5)
  • Anvitha B Ranganna (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (3 events, across 2 seasons)

Tanushri has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Tanushri has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Tanushri brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
tanushri_thokala.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Tanushri Thokala, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-6. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Tanushri has 54 career entries across 21 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Tanushri saga began at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Tanushri would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 8 times Tanushri has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 8 times Tanushri has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Tanushri's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Tanushri plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 54 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 21 of them, to be precise. Tanushri has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Tanushri's texts. The go-to partner is Anvitha B Ranganna (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Mukil Nambikumar has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Tanushri has shown up 8 times with a best of top-6. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-6.

Tanushri has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Tanushri is not just losing locally — Tanushri is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Thokala family travels cross-country so Tanushri can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Tanushri has 22 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Tanushri showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U17, which means Tanushri traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Tanushri entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Tanushri paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 54 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Tanushri. Tanushri just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Tanushri has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Tanushri. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Tanushri: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

tanvi_deshmukh.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Tanvi Deshmukh, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 15 career entries in 6 tournaments, Tanvi has 1 title — a 7% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 14 entries where Tanvi drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 15 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Tanvi saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2021 HPBC NW ORC in GS U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Tanvi's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Tanvi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 1 title came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Tanvi is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner. On the doubles front, Tanvi has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Darron Jaden Su (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U17 — we hope Darron Jaden Su has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Tanvi entered 2 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Tanvi's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Tanvi and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Tanvi 2 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

Tanvi has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Tanvi has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Tanvi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U17, which means Tanvi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 U.S. Selection Event in U17 XD. A pattern, not a blip.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 15 entries, and 14 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Tanvi looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, is still figuring it out, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Tanvi's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Tanvi has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Tanvi. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

tanvi_dhanrajani.name: Tanvi Dhanrajani
tanvi_dhanrajani.summary: Tanvi Dhanrajani is just getting started on the junior circuit with 1 tournament entry. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Tanvi's.
tanvi_dhanrajani.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Tanvi Dhanrajani, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Tanvi is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC, and so far, the only thing Tanvi has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Tanvi saga began at 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC with 1 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Tanvi is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Tanvi's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Tanvi is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Tanvi has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Tanvi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC in GS U15, which means Tanvi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Tanvi is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Tanvi wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Tanvi, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

tanvi_gaddam.name: Tanvi Gaddam
tanvi_gaddam.summary:
Representing Texas, Tanvi Gaddam has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U15 level, reaching the finals 2 times. With 12 entries across 4 tournaments, Tanvi is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

What makes Tanvi especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Tanvi has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 6 different players across the career:
  • Yeewei Yap (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #2)

Tanvi has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Tanvi isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Tanvi's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
tanvi_gaddam.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Tanvi Gaddam, from Texas, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 12 events across 4 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 12-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Tanvi's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Tanvi keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Tanvi saga began at 2023 Midwest ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Tanvi experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Tanvi has reached the finals 2 times — at 2025 Peak Sports South OLC (Dec) — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Tanvi has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Tanvi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 12 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Tanvi has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Yeewei Yap (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 Midwest ORC in XD U13 — we hope Jason Gu has emotionally recovered.

Tanvi has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Tanvi has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Tanvi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in GS U15, which means Tanvi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Tanvi entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Tanvi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Tanvi keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Tanvi has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Tanvi, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Tanvi. We will be here, taking notes.

tanvi_jandhyala.name: Tanvi Jandhyala
tanvi_jandhyala.summary:
Representing Texas, Tanvi Jandhyala broke through with a tournament title, currently competing at the U17 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 56 entries across 19 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Tanvi is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Tanvi is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

Tanvi has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 15 different players across the career:
  • Bhavesh Sai Varma Tammu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

Tanvi has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Tanvi has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Tanvi is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
tanvi_jandhyala.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Tanvi Jandhyala, from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Tanvi has 56 career entries across 19 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 2% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Tanvi saga began at 2023 South ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2025 Schafer SLG South OLC in GD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Tanvi's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 21 entries — a stretch where Tanvi actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Tanvi is 0-for-11. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Tanvi experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Tanvi has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 Austin Leander South OLC, where Tanvi entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GS U15, GD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (1 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Tanvi does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Tanvi has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 1 wins means Tanvi is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Tanvi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Tanvi is 0-for-19. Zero titles in 19 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Tanvi and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Tanvi needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Tanvi has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Tanvi's texts. The go-to partner is Irene Yan (10 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Junwoo Lee has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Tanvi's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Tanvi has shown up 6 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9. Then there is the seeding situation. Tanvi has been seeded in 4 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Tanvi takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2025 Schafer SLG South OLC in GS U15, and finishing 5th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Tanvi. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Tanvi of is playing it safe. Tanvi has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17 — which means Tanvi has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 1 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Tanvi has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Tanvi is not just losing locally — Tanvi is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Jandhyala family travels cross-country so Tanvi can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Tanvi has 32 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Tanvi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U15, which means Tanvi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Tanvi entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Tanvi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 56 entries, and 55 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Tanvi looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Tanvi's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Tanvi has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Tanvi. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

What makes Tanvika especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 11 different doubles partners over the years, Tanvika adapts to anyone:
  • Ayaan Kale (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #3)
  • Diya Abbineni (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (3 events, best: #5)

Tanvika has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Tanvika's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
tanvika_kantipudi.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Tanvika Kantipudi, from Texas, currently competing at the U13 level. A player who has entered 25 events across 9 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 25-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Tanvika's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Tanvika keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Tanvika saga began at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Tanvika plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 25 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Tanvika has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Tanvika's texts. The go-to partner is Ayaan Kale (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11 — we hope Ayushmaan Senapati has emotionally recovered.

Tanvika has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Tanvika has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Tanvika showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U11, which means Tanvika traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Tanvika entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Tanvika paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Tanvika keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Tanvika has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Tanvika, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Tanvika. We will be here, taking notes.

tao_peng.name: Tao Peng
tao_peng.summary:
Hailing from California, Tao Peng is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-3 finish at Selection places Tao in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Notably, Tao has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Tao is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-3 (U15 BD) • Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

With 20 different doubles partners over the years, Tao adapts to anyone:
  • Aarshi Jain (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Mihika Jaiswal (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Kaitlyn Kim (current partner) — currently teaming up in U15/XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Andrew Poh (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #5)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Tao has competed across U11, U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Tao has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Tao is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
tao_peng.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Tao Peng, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-3. Top-3 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 60 career entries in 22 tournaments, Tao has 1 title — a 2% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 59 entries where Tao drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 60 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Tao saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2025 CAN-AM Elite NorCal OLC in BD U15 - Position 1-4, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Tao's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 21 entries — a stretch where Tao actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Tao is 0-for-12. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Tao plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Tao is 0-for-21. Zero titles in 21 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Tao and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Tao needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Tao has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Tao's texts. The go-to partner is Kenneth Wu (10 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Jessica Zhang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Tao's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Tao entered 3 events with results ranging from top-3 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Tao's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Tao and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Tao has shown up 6 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Tao has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Tao is not just losing locally — Tao is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Peng family travels cross-country so Tao can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Tao has 34 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Tao showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U13, which means Tao traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Tao entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Tao paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 60 entries, and 59 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Tao looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Tao's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Tao has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Tao. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

tatthva_sharma.name: Tatthva Sharma
tatthva_sharma.summary:
Representing Texas, Tatthva Sharma has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-3), currently competing at the U17 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Tatthva's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Tatthva has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Tatthva competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (11 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (7 entries) • ORC top-3 finisher.

Tatthva has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 22 different players across the career:
  • Nidhi Sujin (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U17 (12 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Varin Karri (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (6 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Aashi Gupta — a trusted GD/U15 partner (5 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Aarav Bhandary — a trusted U15/XD partner (4 events, spanning 3 seasons)

Tatthva has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Tatthva has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Tatthva brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
tatthva_sharma.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Tatthva Sharma, from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 76 career entries in 28 tournaments, Tatthva has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 75 entries where Tatthva drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 76 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Tatthva saga began at 2021 HPBC NW ORC with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Tatthva plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 76 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 22 of them, to be precise. Tatthva has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Tatthva's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Nidhi Sujin: 12 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 12 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Nidhi Sujin has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Aarav Bhandary has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Tatthva entered 11 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Tatthva's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Tatthva and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Tatthva has shown up 7 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Tatthva has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Tatthva is not just losing locally — Tatthva is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Sharma family travels cross-country so Tatthva can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Tatthva has 37 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Tatthva showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U15, which means Tatthva traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Tatthva entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 65th. That is an average placement of 34th, which is a polite way of saying Tatthva paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 76 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Tatthva. Tatthva just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Tatthva has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Tatthva. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Tatthva: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.
