terence_cai.summary:
Hailing from California, Terence Cai is a Pan American Junior Championships silver medalist, earning a podium finish representing the USA against the best young players in the Western Hemisphere (BD U15, BS U15, XD U15). A top-2 finish at the Pan Am Juniors puts Terence in rarified international company.

Notably, Terence has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Terence especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 4 singles titles, 2 doubles titles, 6 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior silver medalist (BD U15, BS U15, XD U15, 2025) • U.S. Selection Event champion (U13 BD, U13 BS, U15 BD, U15 BS, U15 XD, U13 XD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals top-2 (BS U13, BD U13, BS U11, XD U13, BD U15, XD U11) • 12 ORC titles.

Beyond the trophies, the numbers stand out: Terence boasts a career +795 point differential, 42 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively, a clutch 77% win rate in three-game matches (17/22).

Terence has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 20 different players across the career:
  • Seth Li (current primary partner) — 10 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (11 total events in BD/U15, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Selena Wei (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (4 total events in XD)
  • Saatvik Shetty (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Michelle Yourong Zhang — 9 events in U15/XD, producing 2 titles together, across 2 seasons

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 7 podium finishes across 5 tournaments. Terence is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Terence has competed across U11, U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Terence has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Terence has been seeded #1 or #2 in 18 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 6 seasons of competitive experience and 14 titles, Terence is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
terence_cai.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Terence Cai, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Terence is a Pan American Junior Championships silver medalist (BD U15, BS U15, XD U15), which means Terence has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Terence's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. But here is the thing about a silver medal: it means Terence went all the way to an international championship and STILL did not win. Terence represented an entire country and came home with the consolation prize. Across 97 career entries and a 14% win rate, the Pan Am silver is the crown jewel of a résumé that is otherwise... well, let's just say we have 97 entries worth of material.

The Terence saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Terence's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 7 titles in 26 entries — a stretch where Terence actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 12 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Terence experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Terence has reached 26 championship matches across this career. That is 26 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 26 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 U.S. Selection Event, where Terence entered 3 events and came away with 3 runner-up finishes (U13 BS, U13 XD, U13 BD). 3 chances at gold, 3 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (7 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (12 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Terence does own 14 actual titles, so it is not like Terence has never won anything. But 26 finals losses versus 14 wins means Terence is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Terence plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 4 singles, 2 doubles, 6 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Terence can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Terence has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Terence's texts. The go-to partner is Seth Li (11 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BD U17 — we hope Seth Li has emotionally recovered.

Junior Nationals has been particularly cruel: 11 entries, 4 finals appearances, and zero titles. Terence has stood on the biggest stage in American junior badminton, one match from the most important title in the sport, and come up short 4 times. That is the kind of heartbreak that builds character — or destroys it. Time will tell. Then there is the seeding situation. Terence has been seeded in 30 events and underperformed the seeding 9 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Terence takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BS U17, and finishing 65th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Terence. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Terence has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Terence is not just losing locally — Terence is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 14% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Cai family travels cross-country so Terence can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Terence has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Terence showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BS U17, which means Terence traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 NorCal CRC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 14 titles to the name, 97 entries, and 83 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Pan Am medal, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Terence looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Terence's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Terence has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Terence. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

ternence_liu.name: Ternence Liu
ternence_liu.summary:
Out of New York, Ternence Liu has stepped onto the competitive stage with 2 tournament entries. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Ternence's.

Ternence competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.
ternence_liu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ternence Liu, from New York, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 2 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Ternence is right on schedule. It all started at 2023 NE ORC (U17/U19), and so far, the only thing Ternence has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Ternence saga began at 2023 NE ORC (U17/U19) with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ternence has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ternence showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U17/U19) in BS U19, which means Ternence traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 NE ORC (U17/U19), where Ternence entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 21st, which is a polite way of saying Ternence paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Ternence is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Ternence wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Ternence, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

terrance_jiang.name: Terrance Jiang
terrance_jiang.summary:
Out of Washington, Terrance Jiang has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U11 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 15 entries in 6 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Notably, Terrance has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Terrance especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

With 6 different doubles partners over the years, Terrance adapts to anyone:
  • Roger Kung (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events, spanning 3 seasons)

Terrance has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Terrance has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Terrance brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
terrance_jiang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Terrance Jiang, from Washington, currently competing at the U11 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Terrance has 15 career entries across 6 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Terrance saga began at 2023 South ORC with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Terrance plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 15 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Terrance has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Roger Kung (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-25 exit at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U11 — we hope Terrisa Zhang has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Terrance has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Terrance has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Terrance is not just losing locally — Terrance is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Jiang family travels cross-country so Terrance can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2023 Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Terrance has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Terrance showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U11, which means Terrance traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 Midwest ORC, where Terrance entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Terrance paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Terrance keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Terrance has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Terrance, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Terrance. We will be here, taking notes.

thanik_bharani.name: Thanik Bharani
thanik_bharani.summary:
Representing California, Thanik Bharani has reached the podium at Junior Nationals, finishing top-4 in BD U19. At the biggest event on the calendar, making the final four is a statement: Thanik belongs with the best in the country.

Thanik is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (6 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Thanik is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (9 entries) • Junior Nationals top-4 (BD U19) • 1 ORC title.

With 8 different doubles partners over the years, Thanik adapts to anyone:
  • Ved Vedere (current primary partner) — 13 events, 1 title together in BD/U19, spanning 3 seasons
  • Harshita Kandambakkam (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U19/XD (4 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Jaden Jin (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

Thanik has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Thanik has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Thanik is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
thanik_bharani.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Thanik Bharani, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 48 career entries in 19 tournaments, Thanik has 1 title — a 2% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 47 entries where Thanik drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 48 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Thanik saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 13th. The first title would not come until 2021 HPBC NW ORC in BD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Thanik's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 1 title in 18 entries — a stretch where Thanik actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Thanik is 0-for-12. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Thanik plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Thanik is 0-for-17. Zero titles in 17 tries. The best singles result is a 5th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Thanik and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Thanik needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Thanik has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Thanik's texts. The go-to partner is Ved Vedere (13 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U19 — we hope Harshita Kandambakkam has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Thanik entered 9 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Thanik's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Thanik and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Thanik 5 times, with a best of top-4. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

Thanik has also played up into older age groups at 6 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Thanik is not just losing locally — Thanik is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Bharani family travels cross-country so Thanik can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Thanik has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Thanik showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in BS U17, which means Thanik traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 NorCal CRC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19), where Thanik entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Thanik paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 48 entries, and 47 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Thanik looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Thanik's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Thanik has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Thanik. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

tharun_katharnath.name: Tharun Katharnath
tharun_katharnath.summary:
Hailing from Georgia, Tharun Katharnath has claimed a championship, currently competing at the U15 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 32 entries across 12 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Tharun is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 9 different doubles partners over the years, Tharun adapts to anyone:
  • Sajith Kamal (current primary partner) — 5 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (8 total events in BD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Niya Olivia Chen (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #5)
  • Aradhana Santhosh (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

Tharun is peaking at the right time in 2025-2026, with 1 title across 4 tournaments — a clear sign that Tharun is still ascending.

Tharun has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Over 4 seasons and 1 title, Tharun has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
tharun_katharnath.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Tharun Katharnath, from Georgia, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 1 win across 32 entries in 12 tournaments. That is a 3% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Tharun shows up to a tournament, Tharun pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Tharun saga began at 2023 South CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2025 Fortius South CRC in BD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Tharun's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Tharun plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Tharun is 0-for-12. Zero titles in 12 tries. The best singles result is a 9th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Tharun and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Tharun needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Tharun has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Tharun's texts. The go-to partner is Sajith Kamal (8 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BD U13 — we hope Haotian Deng has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Tharun's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Tharun has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Tharun has 18 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Tharun showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U13, which means Tharun traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15), where Tharun entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Tharun paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 32 entries, and 31 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Tharun looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Tharun's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Tharun has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Tharun. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

thea_yuan.name: Thea Yuan
thea_yuan.summary:
Out of California, Thea Yuan has conquered the Open Regional Championship stage, capturing an impressive 4 ORC titles across 2 regional championships. ORCs are the highest level of regular-season competition, drawing the strongest fields in each region. Winning at this level repeatedly marks Thea as one of the premier players on the circuit.

A true triple-threat, Thea competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 2 singles titles, 2 doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • 4 ORC titles.

Thea has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 7 different players across the career:
  • Timmy Qinnuo Fan (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Neel Achari (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #5)
  • Prarthana Sanghani (current partner) — 2 events in GD, producing 2 titles together

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 4 titles across 5 tournaments — a clear sign that Thea is still ascending.

Thea has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning NE, NW, SoCal, Thea isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Thea's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
thea_yuan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Thea Yuan, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-7. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Thea has 23 career entries across 8 tournaments and has managed to win 4 of them — a 17% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Thea saga began at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in GS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Thea's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 4 titles in 14 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Thea plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 2 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Thea can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. On the doubles front, Thea has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Timmy Qinnuo Fan: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Timmy Qinnuo Fan has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in GD U11 — we hope Elina Xiao-En Yang has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Thea has shown up 3 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Thea has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Thea has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Thea showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in XD U11, which means Thea traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in GD U11. A pattern, not a blip.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 23 entries, and 19 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Thea looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, is still figuring it out, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Thea's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Thea has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Thea. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

theodore_wang.name: Theodore Wang
theodore_wang.summary:
Representing Massachusetts, Theodore Wang has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U15 level, reaching the finals 2 times. With 17 entries across 8 tournaments, Theodore is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

Notably, Theodore has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Theodore especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #4, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 6 different doubles partners over the years, Theodore adapts to anyone:
  • Matthew Zhang (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

Theodore has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Theodore brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
theodore_wang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Theodore Wang, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 17 events across 8 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 17-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Theodore's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Theodore keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Theodore saga began at 2023 MassBad NE OLC with 1 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Theodore experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Theodore has reached the finals 2 times — at 2026 Wayside NE CRC, 2024 NE CRC (NJBC) — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Theodore has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Theodore plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 17 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Theodore has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in XD U13 — we hope Aditi Malladi has emotionally recovered.

Theodore has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Theodore has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Theodore showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U13, which means Theodore traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15), where Theodore entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Theodore paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 17 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Theodore. Theodore just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Theodore has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Theodore. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Theodore: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

thomas_wang.name: Thomas Wang
thomas_wang.summary:
Out of Washington, Thomas Wang is a Regional Championship titlist, currently competing at the U19 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

Notably, Thomas has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Thomas competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 2 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (8 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • 1 ORC title.

Thomas has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Thomas has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 3 titles, Thomas is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
thomas_wang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Thomas Wang, from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 52 career entries in 18 tournaments, Thomas has 3 titles — a 6% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 16 entries where Thomas drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 52 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Thomas saga began at 2021 NW CRC, where Thomas burst onto the scene with a title in BD U17 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Thomas has delivered on some of those promises: 3 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Thomas promised and what Thomas has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 2 titles in 20 entries — a stretch where Thomas actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Thomas is 0-for-2. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Thomas plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Thomas is 0-for-14. Zero titles in 14 tries. The best singles result is a 5th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Thomas and suddenly the wins start appearing — 3 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Thomas needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 22 of them, to be precise. Thomas has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Thomas's texts. The go-to partner is David Purnomo (5 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2023 South ORC in BD U19 — we hope Siva Rajesh has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Thomas entered 8 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Thomas's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Thomas and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Thomas has shown up 3 times with a best of top-5. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-5.

Thomas has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Thomas is not just losing locally — Thomas is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wang family travels cross-country so Thomas can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Thomas has 19 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Thomas showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in BS U17, which means Thomas traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 HPBC NW ORC, where Thomas entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 19th, which is a polite way of saying Thomas paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 52 entries, and 49 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Thomas looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Thomas's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Thomas has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Thomas. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

tian_qi_alan_zhang.name: Tian Qi Alan Zhang
tian_qi_alan_zhang.summary:
Out of California, Tian Qi Alan Zhang is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist — representing the United States on the international stage and coming home with gold. Winning at the Pan Am Junior Championships (Mixed Doubles) is an elite, continent-level achievement. With 10 career titles across 22 tournaments, this is a player who delivers on the biggest stages.

Tian is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Tian competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 5 doubles titles, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior champion (Mixed Doubles, 2025) • U.S. Selection Event champion (U19 XD, U19 BD, U19 BS) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (BD U15) • 3 ORC titles.

Tian has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 15 different players across the career:
  • Youshi Chen (current primary partner) — 6 events together this season alone with 4 titles, building serious momentum (6 total events in BD/U19, across 2 seasons)
  • Audrey Chang (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (5 total events in Mixed/U19/XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Matthew Lei — a trusted BD/U17 partner (6 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Ella Mak — 3 events in U17/XD, best: #4, spanning 3 seasons

The 2024-2025 season tells a story of acceleration, with 5 titles across 6 tournaments — a clear sign that Tian is still ascending.

Tian has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Tian has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 7 seeded entries across the career, Tian consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 10 titles, Tian has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
tian_qi_alan_zhang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Tian Qi Alan Zhang, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Tian is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist (Men's Singles, Mixed Doubles), which means Tian has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Tian's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. A Pan Am gold medal. International champion. The real deal. We would love to end the story right here, but unfortunately there are 59 career entries to discuss and a 17% overall win rate that suggests the Pan Am gold was the peak of a very pointy mountain with a LOT of valley on either side. Let's dig in.

The Tian saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Tian burst onto the scene with a title in BD U15 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Tian has delivered on some of those promises: 10 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Tian promised and what Tian has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. This 2024-2025 season has actually been the best yet — 5 titles in 14 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Tian experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Tian has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Tian does own 10 actual titles, so it is not like Tian has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 10 wins means Tian is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Tian plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 5 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Tian can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Tian has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Tian's texts. The go-to partner is Garret Tan (6 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U17 — we hope Yaorui Shan has emotionally recovered.

One thing nobody can accuse Tian of is playing it safe. Tian has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U15, U17, U19 — which means Tian has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 8 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Tian has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Tian is not just losing locally — Tian is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 17% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhang family travels cross-country so Tian can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Tian has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Tian showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U17, which means Tian traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Tian entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 37th, which is a polite way of saying Tian paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 10 titles to the name, 59 entries, and 49 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Pan Am medal, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Tian looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Tian's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Tian has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Tian. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

tianrui_peng.name: Tianrui Peng
tianrui_peng.summary:
A Illinois competitor, Tianrui Peng has claimed a championship, currently competing at the U19 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 35 entries across 13 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Notably, Tianrui has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Tianrui competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

A veteran of the junior circuit, Tianrui has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Tianrui isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Over 4 seasons and 1 title, Tianrui has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
tianrui_peng.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Tianrui Peng, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Tianrui has 35 career entries across 13 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 3% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Tianrui saga began at 2022 Egret Midwest CRC, where Tianrui burst onto the scene with a title in GD U13 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Tianrui has delivered on some of those promises: 1 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Tianrui promised and what Tianrui has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 1 title in 8 entries — a stretch where Tianrui actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Tianrui is 0-for-6. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Tianrui would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 5 times Tianrui has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 5 times Tianrui has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Tianrui's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Tianrui plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Tianrui is 0-for-13. Zero titles in 13 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Tianrui and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Tianrui needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Tianrui has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Tianrui's texts. The go-to partner is Edison Chen Lin (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Kerrick Linn Hu has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Tianrui's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Tianrui has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Tianrui has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Tianrui has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Tianrui has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Tianrui showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Tianrui traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Tianrui entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Tianrui paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 35 entries, and 34 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Tianrui looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Tianrui's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Tianrui has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Tianrui. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

timmy_qinnuo_fan.name: Timmy Qinnuo Fan
timmy_qinnuo_fan.summary:
Hailing from California, Timmy Qinnuo Fan is a consistent semifinalist, currently competing at the U13 level, reaching the final four 3 times. That kind of reliability at the top of the draw is the signature of a player who will soon be contending for titles.

A true triple-threat, Timmy competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (5 entries).

Timmy has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 14 different players across the career:
  • Thea Yuan (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Devaansh Mahato (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #4)

Timmy has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Timmy has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Timmy brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
timmy_qinnuo_fan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Timmy Qinnuo Fan, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Timmy has 34 career entries across 14 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Timmy saga began at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Timmy plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 34 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Timmy has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Timmy's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Thea Yuan: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Thea Yuan has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Devaansh Mahato has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Timmy has shown up 5 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Timmy is not just losing locally — Timmy is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Fan family travels cross-country so Timmy can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Timmy has 20 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Timmy showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BS U13, which means Timmy traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Timmy entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Timmy paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Timmy keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Timmy has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Timmy, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Timmy. We will be here, taking notes.

timothy_yung_an_chuang.roast:

The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 1 title in 8 entries — a stretch where Timothy actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Timothy is 0-for-3. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Timothy would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Timothy has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Timothy has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Timothy's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Timothy plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Timothy and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. The go-to partner is Jonathan Yung-Nien Chuang (14 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Timothy's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Timothy has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Timothy has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Timothy is not just losing locally — Timothy is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chuang family travels cross-country so Timothy can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Timothy has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Timothy showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 SoCal ORC in BS U15, which means Timothy traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Timothy entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Timothy paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 32 entries, and 31 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Timothy looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Timothy's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Timothy has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Timothy. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.
