Tinghao has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Tinghao showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13, which means Tinghao traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Tinghao entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Tinghao paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 34 entries, and 31 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Tinghao looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Tinghao's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Tinghao has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Tinghao. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

tingxin_tan.name: Tingxin Tan
tingxin_tan.summary:
Hailing from Illinois, Tingxin Tan has claimed a championship, currently competing at the U13 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 14 entries across 6 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Tingxin is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #3, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Tingxin has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 8 different players across the career:
  • Hrishikesh Gollamudi (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in BD

The 2025-2026 season tells a story of acceleration, with 1 title across 3 tournaments — a clear sign that Tingxin is still ascending.

Tingxin has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Tingxin brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
tingxin_tan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Tingxin Tan, from Illinois, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 1 win across 14 entries in 6 tournaments. That is a 7% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Tingxin shows up to a tournament, Tingxin pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Tingxin saga began at 2024 Midwest CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2026 Play N Thrive MW CRC in BD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Tingxin's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Tingxin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Tingxin is 0-for-6. Zero titles in 6 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Tingxin and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Tingxin needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Tingxin has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Tingxin's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in XD U11 — we hope Jayasmi Dhruvaraj has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Tingxin's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Tingxin has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Tingxin has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Tingxin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in XD U11, which means Tingxin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 14 entries, and 13 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Tingxin looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Tingxin's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Tingxin has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Tingxin. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

tirth_brahmbhatt.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Tirth Brahmbhatt, from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Tirth has 14 career entries across 5 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Tirth saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Tirth plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 14 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Tirth has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Veronica Ornatsky (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Veronica Ornatsky has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Tirth has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Tirth has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Tirth has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Tirth showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15, which means Tirth traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Tirth entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Tirth paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Tirth keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Tirth has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Tirth, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Tirth. We will be here, taking notes.

toshan_goswami.name: Toshan Goswami
toshan_goswami.summary:
Representing Massachusetts, Toshan Goswami has claimed a championship, currently competing at the U19 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 33 entries across 14 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Notably, Toshan has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Toshan especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #3, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (7 entries).

Toshan has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 7 different players across the career:
  • Avyukt Iyer — 12 events, 1 title together in BD, spanning 3 seasons
  • Cindy Chen — 2 events in XD, best: #2

Toshan has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Toshan isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Over 4 seasons and 1 title, Toshan has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
toshan_goswami.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Toshan Goswami, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Toshan has 33 career entries across 14 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 3% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Toshan saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) with 3 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2024 NE CRC (NJBC) in BD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Toshan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 1 title in 13 entries — a stretch where Toshan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Toshan is 0-for-1. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Toshan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Toshan is 0-for-14. Zero titles in 14 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Toshan and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Toshan needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Toshan has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Avyukt Iyer (12 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in XD U15 — we hope Yolanda Liu has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Toshan's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Toshan has shown up 7 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Toshan has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Toshan has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Toshan has 18 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Toshan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17, which means Toshan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Toshan entered 3 events with results ranging from 25th to 49th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Toshan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 33 entries, and 32 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Toshan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Toshan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Toshan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Toshan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Notably, Tracy has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

  • Jocelyn Chiang (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (3 total events in GD)

tracy_yu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Tracy Yu, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 4 wins across 46 entries in 16 tournaments. That is a 9% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Tracy shows up to a tournament, Tracy pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Tracy saga began at 2022 SoCal Arena CRC with 1 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2024 SGVBC SoCal CRC in GS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Tracy's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Tracy can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Tracy has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Tracy's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Eddie Sun: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Eddie Sun has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U13 — we hope Eddie Sun has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Tracy's 4 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Tracy has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Tracy is not just losing locally — Tracy is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 9% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yu family travels cross-country so Tracy can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2023 SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Tracy has 19 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Tracy showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 SoCal ORC in GS U13, which means Tracy traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where Tracy entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Tracy paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 46 entries, and 42 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Tracy looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Tracy's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Tracy has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Tracy. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Trilochan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Trilochan's texts. The go-to partner is Naresh Sriram (5 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BD U15 — we hope Ethan Chen has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Trilochan's 5 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Trilochan has shown up 3 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

One thing nobody can accuse Trilochan of is playing it safe. Trilochan has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17 — which means Trilochan has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 5 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Trilochan has built a permanent residence on that line. Trilochan has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Trilochan has 23 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Trilochan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U15, which means Trilochan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15), where Trilochan entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Trilochan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 5 titles to the name, 56 entries, and 51 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Trilochan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Trilochan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Trilochan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Trilochan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

What makes Trisha especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 2 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries) • 1 ORC title.

The numbers tell a compelling story: Trisha boasts a career +583 point differential, a solid 68% win rate over 94 matches, a clutch 74% win rate in three-game matches (20/27).

With 22 different doubles partners over the years, Trisha adapts to anyone:
  • Anshu Gowda (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U13/XD (7 events, best: #2, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Grace Zichen Qu Zhao (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (4 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Vindhya Chekuru (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (3 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Joey Wang (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in XD, across 2 seasons

The 2025-2026 season tells a story of acceleration, with 3 titles across 6 tournaments — a clear sign that Trisha is still ascending.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Trisha has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Trisha has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 6 seeded entries across the career, Trisha consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 4 titles, Trisha is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
trisha_mehta.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Trisha Mehta, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 60 career entries in 20 tournaments, Trisha has 4 titles — a 7% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 14 entries where Trisha drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 60 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Trisha saga began at 2023 Midwest ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2025 NE MassBad OLC in GD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Trisha's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 3 titles in 18 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Trisha experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Trisha has reached 8 championship matches across this career. That is 8 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 8 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 NE ORC (U11), where Trisha entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U11, GD U11). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (5 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Trisha does own 4 actual titles, so it is not like Trisha has never won anything. But 8 finals losses versus 4 wins means Trisha is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Trisha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Trisha can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 22 of them, to be precise. Trisha has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Trisha's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Anshu Gowda: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Anshu Gowda has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U13 — we hope Arnav Satish has emotionally recovered.

Of Trisha's 4 career titles, 3 came at local events versus 1 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Trisha feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Trisha entered 3 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-9. The Selection committee looked at Trisha's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Trisha and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Trisha has shown up 6 times with a best of top-6. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-6.

Trisha has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Trisha is not just losing locally — Trisha is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Mehta family travels cross-country so Trisha can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-49 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Trisha has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Trisha showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U13, which means Trisha traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Trisha entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 49th. That is an average placement of 30th, which is a polite way of saying Trisha paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 60 entries, and 56 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Trisha looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Trisha's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Trisha has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Trisha. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

tsun_wang_lam.summary:
Out of California, Tsun Wang Lam has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U19 level, reaching the finals 1 time. With 50 entries across 18 tournaments, Tsun is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

What makes Tsun especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (6 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

With 14 different doubles partners over the years, Tsun adapts to anyone:
  • Xiaoyue Liu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (6 events, best: #2, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Kameran Hossain (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (8 events, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Kevin Zhao (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U19 (2 events)
  • Ziyan Zhao (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

Tsun has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Tsun has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Tsun brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
tsun_wang_lam.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Tsun Wang Lam, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 50 career entries in 18 tournaments, Tsun has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 49 entries where Tsun drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 50 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Tsun saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Tsun plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 50 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Tsun has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Tsun's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Kameran Hossain: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Kameran Hossain has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in BD U17 — we hope Pradyun Rasamsetti has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Tsun entered 6 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Tsun's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Tsun and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Tsun has shown up 3 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Tsun is not just losing locally — Tsun is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lam family travels cross-country so Tsun can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Tsun has 32 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Tsun showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U17, which means Tsun traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Tsun entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Tsun paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 50 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Tsun. Tsun just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Tsun has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Tsun. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Tsun: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

tsz_wai_lam.name: Tsz Wai Lam
tsz_wai_lam.summary:
Out of California, Tsz Wai Lam has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U17 level, breaking into the semifinals 2 times. Across 20 entries in 9 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Tsz is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a doubles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 7 different doubles partners over the years, Tsz adapts to anyone:
  • Zoe Sun — a trusted XD partner (4 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)

Tsz has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Tsz brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
tsz_wai_lam.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Tsz Wai Lam, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 20 events across 9 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 20-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Tsz's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Tsz keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Tsz saga began at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Tsz plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 20 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Tsz has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Zoe Sun (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U13 — we hope Dean Tang has emotionally recovered.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Tsz has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Tsz showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U13, which means Tsz traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Tsz entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Tsz paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 20 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Tsz. Tsz just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Tsz has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Tsz. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Tsz: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

tvisha_modi.name: Tvisha Modi
tvisha_modi.summary:
A Texas competitor, Tvisha Modi has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (U13 XD, U13 GS, U13 GD) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 9 career titles across 13 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

What makes Tvisha especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 2 doubles titles, 4 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (U13 XD, U13 GS, U13 GD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (XD U13, GD U11 - Position 1-4) • 2 ORC titles.

With 11 different doubles partners over the years, Tvisha adapts to anyone:
  • Rithik Rasamsetti (current primary partner) — 7 events together this season alone with 5 titles, building serious momentum (7 total events in U13/XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Vivian Feng (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U13 (5 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Tvisha has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Tvisha has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 9 seeded entries across the career, Tvisha consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 9 titles, Tvisha is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
tvisha_modi.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Tvisha Modi, from Texas, currently competing at the U13 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Tvisha is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Tvisha has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Tvisha would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Tvisha, we kept reading — all 37 entries across 13 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 24% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Tvisha held up a trophy, there were roughly 3 other times Tvisha walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Tvisha saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Tvisha burst onto the scene with a title in GD U11 - Position 1-4 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Tvisha has delivered on some of those promises: 9 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Tvisha promised and what Tvisha has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 4 titles in 18 entries — a stretch where Tvisha actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 5 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Tvisha experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Tvisha has reached 9 championship matches across this career. That is 9 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 9 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 South ORC, where Tvisha entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U13, GD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Tvisha does own 9 actual titles, so it is not like Tvisha has never won anything. But 9 finals losses versus 9 wins means Tvisha is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Tvisha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 4 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Tvisha can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Tvisha has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Tvisha's texts. The most successful partnership is with Rithik Rasamsetti — 7 events, 5 titles — and honestly, Rithik Rasamsetti deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Rithik Rasamsetti a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Krish Raja has emotionally recovered.

Tournament committees have handed Tvisha a #1 or #2 seed 3 times, and Tvisha has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U11, where Tvisha was seeded #2 and finished 5th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Tvisha is not just losing locally — Tvisha is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 24% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Modi family travels cross-country so Tvisha can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Tvisha has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Tvisha showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in GS U15, which means Tvisha traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Tvisha entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 33rd. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Tvisha paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 9 titles to the name, 37 entries, and 28 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Tvisha looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Tvisha's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Tvisha has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Tvisha. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

tyler_jiang.summary:
A California competitor, Tyler Jiang has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U11 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 12 entries in 5 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Tyler is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Tyler has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 5 different players across the career:
  • Alison Liu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #5)

With tournaments spanning NE, NW, NorCal, Tyler isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Tyler's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
tyler_jiang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Tyler Jiang, from California, currently competing at the U11 level. A player who has entered 12 events across 5 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 12-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Tyler's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Tyler keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Tyler saga began at 2025 OBA NW OLC with 1 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Tyler plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 12 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Tyler has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Alison Liu (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BD U11 — we hope Xintong Darren Lu has emotionally recovered.

Tyler has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Tyler has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Tyler showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BS U11, which means Tyler traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2025 CAN-AM Elite NorCal OLC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Tyler entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 15th, which is a polite way of saying Tyler paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Tyler keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Tyler has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Tyler, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Tyler. We will be here, taking notes.

tyler_xingfei_chen.name: Tyler Xingfei Chen
tyler_xingfei_chen.summary:
Out of Washington, Tyler Xingfei Chen has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U17 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 18 entries in 8 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Notably, Tyler has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Tyler is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (4 entries).

Tyler has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 8 different players across the career:
  • Yunshang Chen (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Tyler has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Tyler brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
tyler_xingfei_chen.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Tyler Xingfei Chen, from Washington, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Tyler has 18 career entries across 8 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Tyler saga began at 2021 NW CRC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Tyler plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 18 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Tyler has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Tyler's texts. The go-to partner is Yunshang Chen (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in BD U15 — we hope Yunshang Chen has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Tyler has shown up 4 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Tyler has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Tyler has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Tyler has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Tyler showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NW ORC in BS U15, which means Tyler traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Tyler entered 2 events with results ranging from 25th to 65th. That is an average placement of 45th, which is a polite way of saying Tyler paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 18 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Tyler. Tyler just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Tyler has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Tyler. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Tyler: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

tyler_yun.name: Tyler Yun
tyler_yun.summary:
Out of New York, Tyler Yun has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U13 level, reaching the finals 1 time. With 21 entries across 10 tournaments, Tyler is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

Tyler is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

Tyler has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Jeremiah Wang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (7 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Constance Y Cai (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

Tyler has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Tyler brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.

tyron_mai.name: Tyron Mai
tyron_mai.summary:
Representing New York, Tyron Mai is on an upward trajectory, currently competing at the U17 level, with 16 entries across 7 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

Notably, Tyron has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Tyron especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Tyron has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Ricky Deng (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Nicoli Liu (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

Tyron has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Tyron brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
